segments.inferno-33-g39: Già is an adverb meaning 'already' or 'by then'. Eran is the archaic/poetic 3rd person plural imperfect of essere (modern erano) meaning 'they were'. Desti is a predicate adjective (plural masculine) meaning 'awake' placed after the copula.
segments.inferno-33-g40: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. L'ora is 'the hour' (l' is the elided article + ora). S'appressava is si appressava, imperfect tense 'was approaching/drawing near' with the reflexive pronoun clitic s' attached. Che introduces a subordinate clause ('when/that'), and cibo is 'food'. Ne here is a clitic referring back to the group (rendered in English as 'to us' or 'for us'); solëa essere is a periphrastic imperfect 'was accustomed to being' (solëa = used to), with essere as auxiliary; addotto is the past participle 'brought'.
segments.inferno-33-g41: E 'and'. Per suo sogno literally 'because of his dream' (per = 'because of' / 'on account of' here); suo is a possessive 'his/its' agreeing with sogno 'dream/vision'. Ciascun is an indefinite pronoun 'each one/everyone'. Dubitava is the imperfect 'was doubting/hesitating/was uncertain' (3rd singular agreeing with ciascun).
segments.inferno-33-g42: E 'and'. Io is the subject pronoun 'I' (emphatic). Senti' is a past tense form (here 'I heard') of sentire; the apostrophe marks the dropped vowel in verse. Chiavar is an infinitive meaning 'to bolt/lock' (infinitive used after senti' with the sense 'I heard ... being bolted'). L'uscio 'the door' (l' = article). Di sotto 'below' or 'on the lower (side)', i.e. 'the lower door'. A l'orribile torre 'at the horrible/dreadful tower' (a + article + noun), indicating location.
segments.inferno-33-g43: Ond' (short for onde/ond) is a conjunctive adverb 'wherefore/thereupon/therefrom', marking consequence. Io 'I'. Guardai is the 1st person singular passato remoto 'I looked' or 'I turned my gaze'. Nel viso 'in the face' or 'into the faces' (nel = in + definite article). A' mie' figliuoi 'at/to my sons' (a = to/at; mie' is the poetic form of mie/my; figliuoi = sons/children). Sanza far motto is an idiomatic negative phrase: sanza = 'without', far = infinitive 'to make/do', motto = 'utterance/sound' — 'without making a sound/without a word'.
segments.inferno-33-g44: Io is the subject pronoun 'I'. Non negates the verb. Piangëa is the archaic/poetic 1st singular imperfect of piangere (note the diaeresis ë) meaning 'I was weeping' — here negated: 'I was not weeping'.
segments.inferno-33-g45: Sì is an adverb 'so' or 'thus' / 'indeed'. Dentro is an adverb 'within' or 'inside'. Impetrai is a rare/poetic 1st person past tense (passato remoto) of impetrare/impetraio used here to mean 'I became hardened/turned hard inwardly' — an internal hardening of feeling, rendered 'I hardened within'.
segments.inferno-33-g46: Piangevan is the 3rd person plural imperfect of piangere (poetic spelling without -o or -ono) meaning 'they were weeping'. Elli is the archaic disjunctive subject pronoun 'they' (used here to emphasize the third person plural subject).
segments.inferno-33-g47: E 'and'. Anselmuccio is a proper name (diminutive form). Mio is the possessive 'my' modifying the name. Disse is the 3rd person singular passato remoto of dire: 'he said'.
segments.inferno-33-g48: Tu is the 2nd person singular pronoun 'you' (vocative here). Guardi is present indicative 2nd person singular of guardare 'you look' or 'you are looking'. Sì is an adverb 'so/ thus' (here an exclamation: 'You look so!').
segments.inferno-33-g49: Padre is the vocative noun 'father' (addressing the speaker).
segments.inferno-33-g50: Che in an exclamatory/question context is the interrogative pronoun 'what'. Hai is the 2nd person singular present of avere 'you have' — idiomatically used as 'what's the matter with you?' or literally 'what have you?'.
segments.inferno-33-g51: Punctuation mark: period marking the end of the line/speech.
segments.inferno-33-g1: La bocca (direct object) appears before the verb for poetic emphasis. sollevò is passato remoto, 3rd-person singular of sollevare (“he lifted/raised”). dal is the contraction of da + il meaning “from the.” fiero is an adjectival qualifier of pasto. The true subject quel peccator follows the verb (inversion typical in poetry): “that sinner (he) lifted the mouth from the fierce meal.”
segments.inferno-33-g2: forbendola is a verbal form with the clitic -la referring back to la bocca; it means “wiping it” (literally ‘forbendo + la’), here presenting a second, simultaneous action to sollevò. a expresses the idea “on/against” the capelli (hairs). del capo is a genitive (“of the head”). ch’ is the relative conjunction che “which/that.” elli is an archaic subject pronoun “he”; avea is the imperfect of avere (“he had”); di retro guasto is an adjectival phrase “spoiled/damaged at the back,” with retro = “behind/back.”
segments.inferno-33-g3: Poi is the temporal adverb “then.” cominciò is passato remoto, 3rd-person singular of cominciare (“he began”), and the colon marks the start of direct speech.
segments.inferno-33-g4: Tu vuo’ is an abbreviated/vulgar form of vuoi (“you want”). ch’io introduces a subordinate clause with rinovelli in the present subjunctive (ch’io rinovelli = “that I should/ought to recount”), used because the main verb expresses desire. disperato modifies dolor (“desperate sorrow/torment”). che introduces a relative clause: ’l cor mi preme is literal “the heart presses me” (preme, present indicative, meaning “weighs on/presses”), mi = “to me,” and già pur pensando means “already, even just by thinking.”
segments.inferno-33-g5: pria ch’ is an archaic temporal conjunction meaning “before.” io is the subject. ne is the pronoun “of it/about it” (referring back to the sorrow/subject matter). favelli is present subjunctive (used after pria ch’) of favellare, here meaning “speak of it / mention it.” The whole clause therefore means “before I speak of it.”
segments.inferno-33-g6: Ma = “but.” se is the conditional/subordinating “if” here (archaic-se); it introduces the hypothetical. le mie parole = “my words/speech.” esser dien is an older construction meaning “are to be / ought to be” (dien = an archaic form of dovere/related auxiliary indicating destiny/necessity). seme = “seed” with metaphorical force. che frutti infamia al traditor = a relative clause: che = “that,” frutti (from fruttare) = “yield/bear” as noun/verb relation “that yields…,” infamia = “infamy,” al traditor = “to the traitor.” ch’i’ rodo: ch’ = “whom/that,” i’ = archaic io “I” (clipped), rodo = present of rodere “I gnaw,” i.e. “whom I gnaw.” The clause as a whole says “if my words will serve as seed that bears infamy upon the traitor I gnaw.”
segments.inferno-33-g7: parlar e lagrimar are infinitives used as the objects of vedrai: “to speak and to weep.” vedrai is futuro semplice, 2nd-person singular of vedere (“you will see”), so the line addresses the listener: “you will see (me) speak and weep together.” insieme = “together/at once.”
segments.inferno-33-g8: Io non so introduces the main clause ('I do not know'). Chi tu se' is an old-word-order way to say 'who you are' (se' = sei, archaic 2nd-person singular of essere). Né introduces the coordinated negative idea 'nor'. Per che modo = 'by what manner/means' (che here = 'what'), and venuto se' is an inverted/periphrastic sequence meaning 'you have come' or 'you are come' (venuto = past participle of venire placed before the verb se' = 'you are'). Qua giù is a fixed adverbial phrase 'down here' (qua = here, giù = down).
segments.inferno-33-g9: Ma introduces a contrast ('but'). Fiorentino is a predicative adjective identifying origin. Mi is a dative/experiencer pronoun ('to me'); sembri is 2nd-person singular present of sembrare ('you seem'). Veramente intensifies the assertion. Quand' is the archaic/poetic form of quando ('when'); io t'odo = 'I hear you' with t' as the clitic direct object 'you' attached to the verb (odo). The subordinate temporal clause 'when I hear you' gives the evidence for the speaker's judgment.
segments.inferno-33-g10: Tu dei saper: 'Tu' is the 2nd-person subject pronoun. 'dei' is an archaic/dialectal form of dovere (modern devi) meaning 'you must' or 'you ought to' and takes the infinitive 'saper' ('to know') as its complement. 'ch’i’ fui conte Ugolino' is a subordinate clause introduced by 'ch’' (che = that); 'i’' is an archaic contracted form of 'io' (I), and 'fui' is the passato remoto of essere ('I was'), so the clause says 'that I was Count Ugolino' with the title in apposition to the subject.
segments.inferno-33-g11: 'e' simply links the clause to the next: 'and'. 'questi' here is a demonstrative/pronominal use meaning 'this one' (that person). 'è' is the present indicative of essere ('is'). 'l’ arcivescovo Ruggieri' names that person; the article is elided before a vowel. The colon marks the identification.
segments.inferno-33-g12: 'or' = 'now' (introducing what follows). 'ti dirò' is future indicative ('I will tell you'): 'ti' = indirect object 'to you', 'dirò' = first-person future of dire. 'perché' here = 'why' (introducing the reason). 'i son' is an archaic contracted subject+verb (i’ = io; son = sono) meaning 'I am'; 'tal vicino' is a predicate phrase: 'tal' = such/so, 'vicino' = near/close, so 'I am so near.'
segments.inferno-33-g13: 'Che' introduces a subordinate/casual clause here (roughly 'because' or 'that'). 'per l’effetto' literally 'by the effect/result', where 'per' = by/through and 'effetto' = result/outcome. 'de’ suo’ mai pensieri' modifies 'effetto': 'de’ suoi' = 'of his', and 'mai pensieri' is a poetic/older phrase meaning many or frequent thoughts/schemes (Dante uses mai in an older sense); 'pensieri' = thoughts/intentions/plots.
segments.inferno-33-g14: 'fidandomi di lui' is a gerundial phrase ('trusting myself to him' / 'trusting him'): 'fidandomi' is the gerund of fidarsi with the clitic 'mi' marking the speaker ('trusting myself' = trusting in myself toward him), 'di' is the preposition 'of/in', and 'lui' is the tonic pronoun 'him'. The whole phrase expresses the circumstance in which the following actions occurred.
segments.inferno-33-g15: 'io' is the subject pronoun 'I'. 'fossi' is an archaic/narrative past form here (Dante uses older forms; functionally in context it reports a past event: 'I was taken'); 'preso' is the past participle 'taken/seized' (passive). 'e poscia morto' joins with 'and afterwards dead/killed' — 'poscia' = afterwards, 'morto' = dead/killed. The clause recounts that the speaker was seized and later died.
segments.inferno-33-g16: 'dir non è mestieri' is an impersonal idiom. 'dir' is the infinitive 'to say'; the construction 'non è mestieri' = 'it is not necessary' (where 'mestieri' = need/necessity). Put together: 'it is not necessary to say (this)'.
segments.inferno-33-g17: 'però' = therefore/so; it introduces what follows as the consequence. 'quel che' = 'that which' (relative phrase). 'non puoi avere inteso' uses the modal 'puoi' + perfect infinitive 'avere inteso' to mean 'you could not have understood/heard' — a polite way to say the addressee lacks that information. Word order is standard for a relative clause after a demonstrative.
segments.inferno-33-g18: 'cioè' = namely / that is (it clarifies what follows). 'come' here introduces a manner clause 'how' (how my death was). 'la morte mia' = 'my death' (noun phrase with possessive after the noun, common in Dante). 'fu' is the passato remoto of essere ('was'); 'cruda' = 'cruel/harsh'.
segments.inferno-33-g19: 'udirai' is the 2nd-person future of udire ('you will hear'); it promises that the listener will be told what was just announced.
segments.inferno-33-g20: 'e saprai' = 'and you will know' (future). 's’ e’' is a cluster of archaic contractions standing for 'se egli' or 'se egli/egli è' (here: 'whether he'), so together the subordinate clause asks 'whether he has offended me.' 'm’ ha offeso' = 'has offended me' (perfect: auxiliary avere + past participle). The whole means 'and you will know whether he has wronged/offended me.'
segments.inferno-33-g21: Breve pertugio dentro da la Muda = 'a short/small hole/opening inside the Muda.' Breve is an adjective modifying pertugio. Dentro da la Muda uses an older prepositional sequence; da + article + proper name indicates location associated with that place (here 'within the Muda'). Word order is adjective + noun + locative phrase.
segments.inferno-33-g22: La qual is a relative pronoun ('which') referring back to la Muda. Per me ha 'l titol de la fame = 'has, for me, the title of famine' (per me = 'to/for me'); ha is present indicative of avere ('it has'). Titol is the noun 'title' and de la fame is a genitive phrase 'of the famine/hunger.' The whole relative clause expresses the speaker's personal characterization of the place.
segments.inferno-33-g23: E che conviene ancor ch'altrui si chiuda: che introduces a subordinate clause. Conviene is present indicative of convenire meaning 'it is fitting/necessary' (impersonal use). Ancor = ancora 'still/yet'. Ch'altrui si chiuda uses impersonal/reflexive si + subjunctive chiuda: 'that others be/should be shut up' (altrui = others). The subjunctive (chiuda) follows conviene + che.
segments.inferno-33-g24: M'avea mostrato is a past perfect construction (avea = imperfect of avere + past participle mostrato) meaning 'had shown me.' Per lo suo forame = 'through its opening' (per = 'through'). Più lune già = 'for more than a moon already' (lune = months); the quantifier più + time noun expresses an amount of time that had already passed.
segments.inferno-33-g25: Quand' io feci 'l mal sonno = 'when I took/made the bad/evil sleep' (feci is the passato remoto of fare used here for a past action). Mal sonno is a fixed phrase meaning a harmful or ominous sleep/dream. Che del futuro mi squarciò 'l velame is a relative clause: squarciò (passato remoto of squarciare) literally 'ripped' or figuratively 'revealed' — here 'it tore the veil of the future for me' (mi = indirect object 'to me'), so the event revealed the future to the speaker.
segments.inferno-33-g26: Questi pareva a me = 'This one seemed to me' (Questi = questo/this one; pareva = imperfect of parere 'seemed'; a me = 'to me'). Maestro e donno are two predicative nouns joined by e, both describing the subject ('master and lord'). The imperfect pareva gives a past, continual or habitual appearance.
segments.inferno-33-g27: Cacciando is a present participle (or gerund-like participial form) meaning 'driving' or 'chasing,' describing an attendant action. Il lupo e 'lupicini' are its objects ('the wolf and the wolf-pups'), and al monte = 'to/up the mountain/hill' marks the direction. Per che introduces a result/explanatory clause ('so that/whereby'); i Pisan (the Pisans) is the subject of vedèr, an infinitive here used with non ponno ('cannot') to mean 'cannot see.' The whole second part expresses consequence: by driving them up the hill, the Pisans cannot see Lucca.
segments.inferno-33-g28: Con is the preposition 'with' introducing an instrumental/associative phrase. 'cagne' is feminine plural (noun) meaning hunting-dogs/hounds; the adjective 'magre' follows the noun and agrees in gender and number (feminine plural) to mean 'lean' or 'thin'.
segments.inferno-33-g29: 'studïose e conte' are adjectives describing the preceding 'cagne' (the trema on ï shows the syllable break). 'studïose' = 'eager/attentive' and 'e' = 'and'. 'conte' is an archaic/ambiguous adjective here (sense not listed in the provided options). The next phrase names the persons: 'Gualandi con Sismondi e con Lanfranchi' (proper names joined by con = 'with'). 's’avea messi' is si + aveva + messi: the reflexive/clitic si (s’) precedes the auxiliary avere (avea, imperfect), plus the past participle messi (masculine plural) — together meaning 'had put/placed themselves' (an archaic compound tense with reflexive sense). 'dinanzi da la fronte' is a locative phrase: dinanzi = 'in front', da la fronte = 'at/from the front/forehead', giving the position 'at the very front'.
segments.inferno-33-g30: 'In picciol corso' is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'in a short course/run' or 'after a short time'. 'mi parieno' = 'seemed to me' (mi = indirect object 'to me' before the verb; parieno = 3rd-person plural form 'they appeared/seemed'). The predicate adjective 'stanchi' follows and agrees with the subject. The subject 'lo padre e ’ figli' ('the father and the sons') comes after the verb, a common poetic word order.
segments.inferno-33-g31: The clause begins with 'e con l’agute scane' ('and with the sharp fangs') where con + definite article + adjective + noun form a prepositional phrase. 'mi parea' ('it seemed to me' / 'appeared to me') uses the impersonal construction with mi as experiencer. 'lor veder fender li fianchi' has infinitives: 'lor' = 'them' (object of veder), 'veder' = 'to see' and 'fender' = 'to split/cleave'; 'li fianchi' is the direct object 'the flanks/sides'. Together: 'it seemed to me to see them split their sides.'
segments.inferno-33-g32: 'Quando' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'fui desto' is a perfect/simple past construction ('I was awake' / 'I woke' — fui = past of essere, desto = adjective 'awake'). 'innanzi la dimane' means 'before the morrow' i.e. before dawn/the next morning; word order places the temporal phrase at the end.
segments.inferno-33-g33: The sequence 'pianger senti’' places the infinitive 'pianger' ('weeping') before the verb 'senti’' (archaic form of 'sentii' / 'I heard'); this is a poetic object-construction meaning 'I heard [them] weeping'. 'fra ’l sonno' = 'amid sleep' specifies where/when. 'i miei figliuoli' is the logical subject of the weeping; the relative clause 'ch’eran con meco' ('who were with me') modifies 'figliuoli'.
segments.inferno-33-g34: The conjunction 'e' links another infinitive object to 'senti’': 'dimandar' = 'to ask' (here 'I heard them asking'), and 'del pane' is the object ('for bread'), with del = di + il indicating 'of/the'. The two infinitives (pianger, dimandar) are both governed by the hearing verb.
segments.inferno-33-g35: 'Ben' is an adverb here intensifying the statement ('truly' / 'indeed'). 'se’' is an archaic second-person present copula ('sei' = 'you are') contracted. 'crudel' is the predicate adjective 'cruel'. The whole phrase is a direct address: 'You are truly cruel.'
segments.inferno-33-g36: This is a conditional subordinate clause introduced by 'se' ('if'). 'tu' is the explicit subject; 'già' = 'already'; 'non ti duoli' = 'you do not grieve' (ti = reflexive pronoun; duoli = present of duolersi). The participial/gerund phrase 'pensando ciò che ’l mio cor s’annunziava' ('thinking of what my heart was proclaiming/foretelling') modifies the state in which the addressee should grieve. 's’annunziava' is reflexive imperfect ('was announcing to itself' or 'was foretelling').
segments.inferno-33-g37: 'e' = 'and' links another conditional. 'se non piangi' = 'if you do not weep' (se = 'if'; piangi = 2nd-person present 'you weep'). This is a direct, rhetorical conditional question setup.
segments.inferno-33-g38: 'di che' = 'for what' or 'about what'. 'pianger' is the infinitive 'to weep' used here as the verbal idea governed by 'suoli' (archaic 2nd-person singular of 'suolare' = 'to be accustomed'), so the whole question asks 'what do you usually weep for?'. The provided noun senses for 'suoli' do not fit its verbal use here, so that sense is marked unknown.
segments.inferno-33-g138: The interjection “Oh” expresses surprise or emotion and stands alone in quotes before the reported speech; it requires no grammatical connection to the following clause.
segments.inferno-33-g139: diss' is an apocopated/poetic form of the past tense 'dissi' (I said); the verb comes before the subject ('io') for stylistic inversion, and 'lui' functions as the indirect object ('to him').
segments.inferno-33-g140: This is a direct question inside quotes. 'or' = 'now'; 'se'' is an archaic/poetic form of the 2nd-person singular of 'essere' ('sei' → 'are'); 'tu' is the explicit subject pronoun; 'ancor' = 'still'; 'morto' is the past participle used adjectivally meaning 'dead' (here describing the state of 'tu').
segments.inferno-33-g141: A single full stop (period) marking the end of the previous quoted question — punctuation only, no lexical content to analyse.
segments.inferno-33-g142: 'Ed' = 'and' (conjunction). 'elli' is an archaic form of 'egli' meaning 'he' (here the subject), and 'a me' = 'to me' (dative) — the clause is an introductory formula before direct speech, with the verb 'said' understood rather than repeated.
segments.inferno-33-g143: Inside the quotation: 'Come' = 'how' introducing an indirect question; '’l' = the definite article 'il' before 'mio corpo'; 'mio corpo' = 'my body'; 'stea' is an archaic/poetic present subjunctive (or archaic present) of 'stare' meaning 'is situated' or 'stands'; 'nel' = 'in the'; 'mondo sù' = 'world above' (sù = 'above').
segments.inferno-33-g144: 'Nulla' = 'no' or 'nothing' negating the following noun; 'scïenza' = 'knowledge' (here 'awareness' of the state of his body); 'porto' is the 1st person singular present of 'portare' used idiomatically 'I bear/have' — together 'nulla scïenza porto' = 'I have no knowledge.'
segments.inferno-33-g145: 'Cotal' is an archaic/poetic form of cotale, meaning 'such' and placed before the noun for emphasis; 'vantaggio' is the noun 'advantage' (direct object fronted for stylistic inversion). The verb 'ha' is present indicative of avere ('has'), and 'questa Tolomea' is the subject placed after the verb (inverted order common in poetry) meaning 'this Tolomea/this Ptolemaea'.
segments.inferno-33-g146: The relative/result clause begins with 'che' = 'that'. 'Innanzi ch' (= innanzi che) means 'before' and here is followed by an elliptical, poetic construction: instead of a full finite verb the past participle 'mossa' ('moved') appears with 'le dea' ('the goddess'), so the clause effectively means 'before the goddess Atropos is moved' (the auxiliary 'sia' or similar is omitted as a poetic ellipsis). segments.inferno-33-g73: This segment contains only punctuation (a period) and carries no lexical material to analyze.
segments.inferno-33-g74: 'Quivi' is a literary locative adverb meaning 'there'; 'morì' is the passato remoto (third person singular) of 'morire' meaning 'died'. The subject is omitted (he/it) as is common in Italian poetry; the semicolon connects this short clause to what follows.
segments.inferno-33-g75: 'e' is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking to the previous clause; 'come' introduces a comparison ('as'); 'tu' is the explicit 2nd-person subject; 'mi vedi' is present indicative (2nd person singular) meaning 'you see me' — here it refers to the addressee's present perception. segments.inferno-33-g76: 'Vid'' is a syncopated poetic form of 'vidi' (passato remoto 1st sing.) meaning 'I saw', with the subject 'io' following the verb (inverted order common in verse). 'Cascar' is the infinitive used after a perception verb ('I saw ... fall'); 'li tre' is the definite article + numeral 'the three' (object of the infinitive); 'ad uno ad uno' = 'one by one'; 'tra 'l quinto dì e 'l sesto' is a temporal phrase 'between the fifth day and the sixth'.
segments.inferno-33-g77: 'Ond'' (short for 'onde') is a result/causal connective here meaning 'whereupon' or 'therefore'; 'io' is the subject; 'mi diedi' is passato remoto reflexive of 'dare' + 'mi' meaning 'I gave myself' or 'I set myself' (to a task). The verb introduces what he devoted himself to, which continues in the next segment ('a brancolar').
segments.inferno-33-g78: 'Già' is an adverb meaning 'already' emphasizing the state; 'cieco' is an adjective 'blind'. Placed between commas it is a parenthetical description of his condition at the time he 'gave himself' to the action named next.
segments.inferno-33-g79: 'A' + infinitive (a brancolar) indicates purpose or the action undertaken: 'to grope' or 'to fumble about'; 'sovra' is a preposition here meaning 'over' or 'above'; 'ciascuno' means 'each one' (each of them). The phrase completes the purposive construction begun by 'mi diedi'.
segments.inferno-33-g80: 'E' = 'and' connects another action; 'due dì' is a temporal expression 'for two days' (word order pre-verbal for emphasis); 'li' is the clitic object pronoun 'them'; 'chiamai' is passato remoto (1st sing.) of 'chiamare' meaning 'I called' or 'I cried out to' them. In poetic narrative the time expression can precede the verb without an auxiliary.
segments.inferno-33-g81: 'Poi che' here functions as a temporal conjunction 'after (that)'; 'morti' is the past participle/plural adjective 'dead'. Together they mean 'after they were dead' and subordinate to the previous clause (why he called to them was to no avail because they were dead).
segments.inferno-33-g82: 'Poscia' is an adverb meaning 'afterwards' or 'then' and sets the temporal frame for the clause that follows; the comma marks a slight pause before the emphasis on cause or agent.
segments.inferno-33-g83: 'Più che' is a comparative construction meaning 'more than'; here it contrasts 'the pain' with what follows. The article 'l'' (= 'the') attaches to 'dolor' (pain/sorrow).
segments.inferno-33-g84: 'Poté' is the passato remoto (3rd singular) of 'potere' and in this context means 'prevailed' or 'had power'; the subject follows in inverted order: '’l digiuno' ('the fasting') — thus 'fasting prevailed.' This is a concise, poetic inversion common in Dante.
segments.inferno-33-g85: 'Quand'' is the elided form of 'quando' meaning 'when'; 'ebbe detto' is a literary perfect/passato remoto compound ('had said' or 'said') formed with 'avere' + past participle 'detto' and places the action prior to the main verb that follows; 'ciò' is the demonstrative 'this/that' as object of 'detto'.
segments.inferno-33-g86: 'Con' introduces an accompanying circumstance ('with'); 'li occhi' = 'the eyes'; 'torti' here means 'twisted' or 'askew' (the provided lemma list is incorrect for this form, so the precise sense is given in the explanation). 'Riprese' is passato remoto of 'riprendere' meaning 'he took again' or 'he resumed/seized again'; '’l teschio misero' = 'the wretched skull'; 'co’ denti' is a contracted form ('con i denti') meaning 'with his teeth'.
segments.inferno-33-g87: 'Che' is a relative pronoun referring back to 'denti' ('which'); 'furo' is an archaic/passé remoto plural of 'essere' ('they were'); 'a l'osso' = 'to the bone' (i.e., down to the bone). The clause describes the extent or force of the teeth's action.
segments.inferno-33-g88: 'Come' introduces a simile 'like'; 'd'' is the contracted preposition 'di' ('of'/'like'); 'un' is the indefinite article 'a'. Together with the omitted final noun in this segment (can = 'dog' appears in the line but is not listed in the words array), the phrase makes the comparison 'like a dog'.
segments.inferno-33-g89: 'Forti' is an adjective in the plural agreeing with 'denti' ('strong'). Placed after the simile it emphatically qualifies the teeth as 'strong', completing the pictorial description.
segments.inferno-33-g177: Ché is a coordinating conjunction (poetic/archaic spelling of che) meaning 'for' or 'because' and here introduces the speaker's explanation. Col is the contraction of con + il ('with the') and governs the following noun phrase; peggiore is an attributive adjective ('vilest/worst') modifying spirto (an archaic form of spirito, 'spirit' or 'soul'). Trovai is passato remoto, 1st person singular ('I found/met'), and the phrase di voi un tal is partitive/identifying ('one such of you' = 'one of you, such a one'), with di voi marking the group he belongs to.
segments.inferno-33-g178: Che is a relative pronoun ('who/which') referring back to un tal. Per sua opra means 'by his own deed/action' (per = 'by/through', sua = 'his', opra = 'deed/work'). In anima in Cocito già si bagna literally 'in (his) soul in Cocytus already bathes himself': the reflexive si plus bagna (3rd person singular present) indicates that his soul is already bathing in Cocytus as a result of his action; già = 'already' places this state in effect at the time of narration.
segments.inferno-33-g179: E is the conjunction 'and' linking this clause to the previous. In corpo ('in [the] body') locates the remaining, visible part of the person; par is a poetic contraction of pare ('seems/appears', 3rd person singular present) and vivo means 'alive.' Ancor ('still') emphasizes that his body yet appears living, and di sopra ('above'/'up top') specifies that this apparent life is on the surface/above ground — the implied subject is the same un tal mentioned earlier.
segments.inferno-33-g90: Ahi is an interjection of lament (vocative) addressing the city; Pisa is the vocative proper name. Together they open the apostrophe to the city.
segments.inferno-33-g91: Vituperio is a noun meaning 'reproach/disgrace' and is the head of the noun phrase; de le genti = 'of the peoples' (de = of + definite article le). Del bel paese = 'of the fair country/land' (del = di + il), and là dove 'l sì suona is a locative relative clause 'there where the “yes” is sounded' (’l = il before sì). The whole phrase apposes and specifies who finds Pisa shameful.
segments.inferno-33-g92: Poi che introduces a causal clause 'since/because'; i vicini = 'the neighbors' (subject); a te = 'to you'; punir is the infinitive 'to punish' used with son lenti ('are slow') – son is the archaic 3rd‑person plural/present of essere, lenti is the adjective 'slow'. The clause means 'since the neighbors are slow to punish you.'
segments.inferno-33-g93: Muovasi is a jussive/imperative/subjunctive form (verb + enclitic -si) meaning 'let ... move' or 'may ... move' with the subject following for poetic inversion; la Capraia e la Gorgona are the two island-names (subjects). Word order is verb + subject for forceful exhortation.
segments.inferno-33-g94: E faccian is 3rd‑person plural present subjunctive of fare ('let them make/let them do'); siepe is the direct object 'a hedge/barrier'. Ad Arno in su la foce = 'at the Arno, up at the mouth' with in su = 'up at/toward' and la foce = 'the mouth' (the river mouth). The phrase orders them to form a barrier at the river mouth.
segments.inferno-33-g95: Sì ch’ is a contraction of sì che meaning 'so that' (introducing result/purpose). Elli = 'they' (referring to the islands); annieghi is a subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may drown' or resultive 'drown' (Dante's spelling/ending differs from modern Italian); in te = 'in you' (in your waters/territory); ogne persona = 'every person'. The clause expresses the wished-for consequence: that all persons be drowned in you.
segments.inferno-33-g96: Che se = 'for if' introducing a conditional. ’l conte Ugolino = 'the Count Ugolino' (subject). Aveva voce = literally 'had a voice' but idiomatically 'had a reputation/there was talk' (voce = fame/reputation). D’aver tradita te = a perfect infinitive 'of having betrayed you' (aver + past participle tradita) functioning as the content of 'voce'; de le castella = 'concerning the castles'. The clause means 'if Count Ugolino had the reputation of having betrayed you about the castles.'
segments.inferno-33-g97: Non dovei tu = 'you ought not' with inversion (non + modal dovei + subject tu); dovei is an archaic/poetic form of dovere expressing obligation ('ought to' or 'should have'). I figliuoi = 'the sons' (direct object); porre = infinitive 'to place/put'; a tal croce = 'to such a cross' (tal = such, croce = cross/burden/punishment). Together: 'you should not have put your sons to such a cross.'
segments.inferno-33-g98: Innocenti is a predicate/complement meaning 'innocent' (here plural) and facea is the imperfect of fare 'made/was making'; l’età novella = 'the new age' (l' = the). The line uses inverted order: 'The new age made them innocent.'
segments.inferno-33-g99: Novella Tebe is an appositive phrase 'a new Thebes' (novella = new/renewed; Tebe = Thebes). The construction restates or intensifies the image of a renewed era of naïveté/innocence.
segments.inferno-33-g100: This is a catalogue: Uguiccione e ’l Brigata = 'Uguiccione and the Brigata' (’l = il); e li altri due = 'and the other two' (li = the, altri = others). Che l canto suso appella is a relative clause 'whom the song up there calls/names' (che = who/whom; l canto = the song; suso = up there/above; appella = calls/names). Word order and article usage reflect Dante's archaic/poetic norms.
segments.inferno-33-g101: Noi = 'we' (subject); passammo is passato remoto 'we passed' (a completed past action); oltre = 'beyond' or 'onward'. Simple narrative clause: 'We passed on.'
segments.inferno-33-g102: Là ’ve = 'there where' (ve = archaic locative 'where'); la gelata = 'the frost/icy crust'; ruvidamente = adverb 'harshly/roughly'; un’altra gente = 'another band/group of people'; fascia here is used as a verb (from fasciare) 'wraps/encircles' — the frozen crust encloses them. Word order places the agent (la gelata) before the verb and object for poetic effect.
segments.inferno-33-g103: Non volta in giù = 'not turned downward' (non = negation; volta from volgere/volta = 'turned' used as participle/adjectival); in giù = 'downwards/face down'. The phrase describes the orientation of those encased.
segments.inferno-33-g104: Ma = 'but'; tutta = 'all/entirely'; riversata is a past participle used adjectivally 'overturned/laid back' (from riversare) — together 'but all overturned' or 'but entirely overturned.'
segments.inferno-33-g105: "Lo pianto stesso lì pianger non lascia," has subject + adverb + verb + infinitive construction. "Lo" is the archaic singular article (= modern il) marking the noun "pianto" (a nominalized form of piangere, 'weeping'); "stesso" is emphatic ('very'/'same'). "lì" is a locative adverb 'there'. "pianger" is the infinitive complement of "lascia" (lasciare + infinitive = 'let/allow'); the negative "non" negates "lascia" so the clause means 'that very weeping there does not allow one to cry (out)'.
segments.inferno-33-g106: "e ’l duol che truova in su li occhi rintoppo," is a coordinate clause. "e" = 'and'; "’l duol" = 'the sorrow'. "che" is a relative pronoun introducing the clause whose verb is "truova" (Dante's older form of trova/treuova), here used as an indicative 'finds/encounters'. "in su li occhi" is a prepositional phrase 'upon the eyes' and "rintoppo" is the direct object/predicate meaning 'an obstacle' — 'the sorrow that encounters an obstacle on the eyes'.
segments.inferno-33-g107: "si volge in entro a far crescer l’ambascia;" — "si volge" is pronominal/reflective 'it turns' (the subject is the previously named 'duol'). "in entro" (= dentro) intensifies direction 'inward/within'. The infinitival sequence "a far crecer" is a causative periphrasis: "far" (to make) + infinitive "crescer" (to grow) gives 'to cause to grow'; "l’ ambascia" is the direct object ('the anguish'). Overall: 'it turns inward to make the anguish increase.'
segments.inferno-33-g108: "ché le lagrime prime fanno groppo," — "ché" = 'for/because'. Subject "le lagrime prime" ('the first tears') is plural and governs the present verb "fanno" ('make/form'). "groppo" is a noun meaning 'knot/lump' used figuratively: 'the first tears form a lump (in the throat)'.
segments.inferno-33-g109: "e sì come visiere di cristallo," is a simile introduced by "sì come" = 'and just as/and like'. "visiere" (pl.) 'visors' or 'face-pieces' is metaphorical, and "di cristallo" qualifies them ('made of crystal'). This phrase modifies the following verb: the tears behave like crystal visors.
segments.inferno-33-g110: "rïempion sotto ’l ciglio tutto il coppo." — "rïempion" (Dante's older spelling of riempiono/riempiono or riempieron) is a third-person plural verb 'they filled/fill' whose subject is the preceding "visiere di cristallo"; it is used as a simple present/pluperfective third-person plural in Dante's style. "sotto 'l ciglio" = 'beneath the eyelash/under the eyelid'; "tutto il coppo" = 'the whole cup/hollow' (the bowl-like hollow of the eye). So: 'they (the crystal visors) filled the whole cup beneath the eyelash.'
segments.inferno-33-g111: "E avvegna che," — "E" = 'and'. "avvegna" is the impersonal/3rd-person singular form of avvegnare ('to happen'/'befall') and here introduces the subordinate clause; with "che" it marks 'it happened that...' or in context a concessive/time framing (Dante's "avvegna che" often means 'it happened that' / 'although').
segments.inferno-33-g112: "sì come d’un callo," — a simile: "sì come" = 'just as'. "d’un callo" = 'of a (skin) callus'; the image is that a hardened feeling (like a callus) has been formed, so sensation is blunted. Word order is straightforward simile.
segments.inferno-33-g113: "per la freddura ciascun sentimento cessato avesse del mio viso stallo," — a causal/subordinate clause. "per la freddura" = 'because of the cold'. The verbal sequence "cessato avesse" is a past perfect subjunctive (past participle + imperfect subjunctive auxiliary) used here because the clause depends on the impersonal/concessive frame set by "avvegna che"; it means 'had ceased'. "ciascun sentimento" is the subject 'each feeling', and "del mio viso stallo" is a poetic locution 'from the seat/place of my face' (i.e. facial sensation).
segments.inferno-33-g114: "già mi parea sentire alquanto vento;" — "già" = 'already'. "mi parea" is the imperfect of "parere" with dative pronoun 'to me' and means 'it seemed to me' or 'I seemed/to me it appeared'. The infinitive "sentire" is the complement: 'to feel/hear', so "mi parea sentire" = 'it seemed to me to feel'. "alquanto vento" = 'somewhat of a wind' / 'some breeze'.
segments.inferno-33-g115: "per ch’io:" — contraction of "per che io" meaning 'wherefore I' or 'so I', introducing the speaker's following speech. Grammatically it links the preceding perception to the reaction that follows.
segments.inferno-33-g116: "Maestro mio," — a direct vocative address. "Maestro" (master/teacher/guide) is being called by the speaker and "mio" is the possessive in vocative position: 'my master.' Word order is typical for direct address.
segments.inferno-33-g117: "questo chi move?" — an interrogative clause. "questo" is the demonstrative subject 'this', "chi" is the interrogative 'who' (used here to ask 'who/what sets this in motion'), and "move" (Dante's form of muovere) is third-person singular present 'moves/sets in motion'. The order places the demonstrative first for emphasis: 'Who moves this?'
segments.inferno-33-g118: "non è qua giù ogne vapore spento?" — a negative interrogative with copula. "non è" = 'is not/aren't', "qua giù" = 'here below/down here' (locative adverbial), "ogne vapore" = 'every vapor' (ogne = every), and "spento" is the past participle used adjectivally 'extinguished'. The clause literally asks whether all vapors are extinguished in this lower place.
segments.inferno-33-g119: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a period) that closes the preceding line; it has no lexical or grammatical content to analyze.
segments.inferno-33-g52: Perciò is an adverb meaning 'therefore' introducing the reason for what follows. Non negates the verbs that follow; lagrimai is passato remoto (1st person singular) of 'lagrimare' = 'I wept'. Né is the coordinating negative 'nor' linking the two negated actions; rispuos’ io is an inverted, elided form of rispuosi/io risposi (passato remoto of 'rispondere'), with the enclitic apostrophe showing missing vowel and the pronoun io placed after the verb for poetic inversion. Tutto quel giorno is a temporal adverbial ('all that day') with tutto intensifying quel giorno; né la notte appresso places another negated time period ('nor the night following'), appresso = 'after' or 'following'.
segments.inferno-33-g53: Infin che is a temporal conjunction meaning 'until'; che introduces the subordinate clause. L’altro sol is a poetic phrase ('the other sun' = the next day's sun), with the elided article l' + altro + sol (sol = sun). Nel mondo is the locative phrase 'in the world'; uscìo is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of 'uscire' used here in sense 'emerged/rose'.
segments.inferno-33-g54: Come at the start functions as 'as' or 'when'. Un poco di raggio = 'a little ray' (poco modifies raggio; di links them). Si fu messo is a reflexive/passive-like construction: si (reflexive pronoun) + fu (passé remote/auxiliary 'be') + messo (past participle of mettere) = 'was put' or 'was cast'. Nel doloroso carcere is a locative phrase 'into/within the dolorous prison'; this whole clause indicates a temporal point when what follows happens.
segments.inferno-33-g55: E io scorsi: the connective e 'and' links to the next action; io is topicalized subject; scorsi is passato remoto (1st person) of 'scorgere'/'scorrere' meaning 'I perceived/noticed/discerned'. Per quattro visi functions as an instrument/medium ('through/over four faces') and il mio aspetto stesso (il + mio + stesso) literally 'my very appearance' — stesso intensifies as 'the same/very'. Word order (per + measure + object) is poetic but straightforward.
segments.inferno-33-g56: Ambo le man = 'both hands' (ambo is an archaic/poetic for 'both' placed before the noun). Per lo dolor is a causal phrase 'because of the pain' (per + definite article + noun); mi is the clitic object pronoun 'me/myself' placed before the verb morsi, which is passato remoto (1st person sing.) of mordere = 'I bit'. The clause thus reads 'with both hands, because of the pain, I bit myself.'
segments.inferno-33-g57: Ed = and (e with vowel elision before a vowel); ei is an archaic subject pronoun here meaning 'he' (one of the other speakers) and introduces his reaction. The comma marks the pause before the gerund clause describing his thought.
segments.inferno-33-g58: Pensando is a gerund ('thinking'); ch’ is the elided form of che introducing a content clause. Io is the explicit subject pronoun (emphatic). ’l is clitic for lo (him/it) referring back to the biting action; fessi is an archaic/poetic verbal form (here a past subjunctive/imperfect-subjunctive or similar) meaning 'I had done' or 'I had made' — Dante uses older inflectional forms. Per voglia di manicar = 'from/through desire to gnaw/eat' (per voglia = 'from desire', di + infinitive manicar = 'to eat/gnaw').
segments.inferno-33-g59: Di sùbito is an adverbial phrase meaning 'suddenly/at once' (di combines with sùbito). Levorsi is a reflexive past form (si + levò) here in passato remoto meaning 'he rose up' or 'he got up' (the -rsi ending is the old enclitic). E disser is the past historic 'and said' (disser = passar remoto of dire used in third person plural/poetic forms).
segments.inferno-33-g60: Padre is the direct vocative 'Father' addressed to the speaker; in poetry the vocative appears without special marking. This begins the quoted speech.
segments.inferno-33-g61: Assai ci fia men doglia is a future/fictive construction: assai = 'much' or 'very'; ci = 'for us' or 'to us' (indirect object); fia is an archaic/fixed future form from 'essere' ('there will be'); men = 'less'; doglia = 'grief/pain' (noun). Se tu mangi di noi = conditional temporal clause 'if you eat of us' with tu mangi as present subjunctive/indicative used in conditional sense and di noi meaning 'from us/of us'.
segments.inferno-33-g62: Tu ne vestisti is perfect/past (vestisti = passato remoto of vestire) with tu subject; ne is a clitic meaning 'of them/from them' or 'with them' depending on construction, here linking 'you' to what you clothed. Queste misere carni is the direct object 'these miserable bodies/flesh' (queste = these, misere = wretched, carni = bodies/flesh). The clause accuses the addressee of having clothed those bodies.
segments.inferno-33-g63: E tu le spoglia is a short clause continuing the address: e = and; tu emphatic subject; le is an object clitic 'them' (referring to the carni) placed before spoglia. Spoglia here functions as a verb form (present indicative) 'you strip' or 'you lay bare' in the sense 'you strip them'. The sentence is direct speech and uses simple, pointed verb forms.
segments.inferno-33-g64: Queta’mi allor is an archaic/poetic contraction: queta' = quetai/quetai (I calmed) with enclitic mi = 'myself', allor = 'then'. Per non farli più tristi is a purpose/intent clause 'in order not to make them more sad' (per non + infinitive fare with direct object clitic li in farli; più tristi = more sad). Word order places the reflexive after the verb in the contracted form.
segments.inferno-33-g65: Lo dì e l’altro = 'the day and the next' (lo dì = the day; l'altro = the other/next), stemmo is passato remoto/poetic past of 'stare'/'rimanere' in 1st person plural = 'we remained/stood'; tutti muti = 'all silent/mute'. The clause reports their continued silence over the day and following day.
segments.inferno-33-g66: Ahi is an interjection 'ah' or 'alas'; dura terra = 'hard/unyielding earth' (adjective before noun as usual). This is an exclamatory apostrophe to the earth.
segments.inferno-33-g67: Perché non t’apristi? is a direct question: perché = 'why'; non negates the verbal form; t' is the clitic 'yourself/you' attached to apristi; apristi is a past-tense form (passato remoto or archaic subjunctive) of aprire 'to open' in the 2nd person singular 'you opened' — here used in the sense 'why did you not open yourself?' (a plea for the earth to open). The pronoun is enclitic to the verb in this older form.
segments.inferno-33-g68: Poscia che fummo al quarto dì venuti is a temporal clause: poscia che = 'after that/after', fummo venuti is passato remoto/perfect ('we had come' / 'we came') with fummo (auxiliary) + venuti (past participle), al quarto dì = 'on the fourth day'. The clause situates the following action in time.
segments.inferno-33-g69: Gaddo mi si gittò disteso a’ piedi: Gaddo (proper name) is subject; mi is a dative/clitic indicating direction toward 'me' (he threw himself down at me); si gittò is reflexive passato remoto of gittare/se giettare ('he threw himself down'); disteso = 'stretched out' (past participle used adjectivally); a’ piedi = 'at (my) feet' (poetic contraction a' = ai/at the). The word order places the reflexive pronoun and object clitics as Dante typically does.
segments.inferno-33-g70: Dicendo is a present participle/gerund 'saying', introducing the reported speech that follows; in Italian it often follows the main verb to express simultaneous action.
segments.inferno-33-g71: Padre mio is the vocative 'my father' (Padre = father; mio = my) addressing the narrator; the noun + possessive forms the direct address.
segments.inferno-33-g72: Ché non m’aiuti? is a colloquial/poetic question: ché here functions as 'why' (an alternative to perché); non negates the verb; m' is the clitic for 'me' (object), and aiuti is present subjunctive/indicative form of 'aiutare' used in interrogative 'why don't you help me?'. The compact clitic placement (m' before the verb) is standard in older Italian.
segments.inferno-33-g163: Nel is the contracted preposition + article (in + il) meaning "in the." Fosso is a masculine noun meaning "ditch/pit" and sù is an adverb placed after the noun to indicate upward/above — together giving a fixed locative phrase "up in the ditch."
segments.inferno-33-g164: Diss' is the literary past tense of dire (diss' = disse with the dropped final vowel), here reported speech marker "he said." El is the archaic object/subject pronoun for "he" (modern Italian sarebbe egli or lui); word order 'diss' el' is inverted for verse.
segments.inferno-33-g165: De' is the contracted form of di + i (of the) used before a plural name; Malebranche is the proper name of the demon-band (literally "Evil-claws"). Together they mark the location as "of the Malebranche."
segments.inferno-33-g166: Là dove is the locative "there where" (là = there, dove = where). Bolle is present indicative of bollire "to boil," describing the place; la tenace pece is the noun phrase "the tenacious/sticky pitch/tar," where tenace is an adjective modifying pece. Adjective follows the noun phrase order typical in poetry.
segments.inferno-33-g167: Non era ancora giunto is a past tense description: era giunto = "had arrived" (verbal periphrasis with essere + past participle giunto), negated by non and modified by ancora meaning "yet" (non era ancora giunto = "had not yet arrived"). Michel Zanche is the proper name of the man referenced.
segments.inferno-33-g168: Che here introduces the following clause in the temporal/causal sense ("when/before/that"). The sequence has an object-first inversion: questi ("this one") is the direct object, lasciò is the past-tense verb "left," and il diavolo is the subject placed after the verb for poetic inversion — so 'the devil left this one.' In sua vece = 'in his stead' and nel corpo suo repeats/location phrase 'in his body.'
segments.inferno-33-g169: Ed = 'and.' Un suo prossimano uses un + suo (a/one + his) and prossimano here denotes a near companion/neighbor (poetic/archaic noun). Che introduces a relative clause 'who' (che = who/that) modifying prossimano; 'l' is the clitic article for 'the' before tradimento; insieme con lui fece is the past tense 'did/made together with him' — i.e. 'who together with him committed the treachery.'
segments.inferno-33-g170: Ma = 'but.' Distendi is the second-person singular imperative (or present indicative used imperatively) of distendere, here meaning 'stretch out/extend' and taking la mano as its object. Oggimai (oggimai/oggimai) is an adverb meaning 'by now/now,' and in qua = 'to here/this way' places the motion toward the speaker.
segments.inferno-33-g171: Aprimi is the imperative/aphetic form apri + mi (open + to me) meaning 'open for me' or 'open (your) eyes for me' — the clitic mi attached to the imperative. Li occhi is the direct object 'the eyes' (li = plural article; occhi = eyes). In the verse aprimi li occhi, the speaker commands the addressee to open their eyes to the speaker.
segments.inferno-33-g172: E io non gliel' apersi: E = 'and,' io = 'I.' Non negates the verb; gliel' is the fused clitic gli + lo (to him it / for him them) referring to 'them' (the eyes) plus indirect object 'to him.' Apersi is the first-person singular past indicative of aprire (apersi = I opened), so non gliel' apersi = 'I did not open them to him.'
segments.inferno-33-g173: E cortesia fu lui esser villano: e = 'and.' Cortesia 'courtesy/favor' is predicate noun; fu is the past tense 'was' (third-person singular of essere). Lui is the dative/emphatic 'on his part' here; esser villano is the infinitive 'to be a boor' used as the complement: 'it was courtesy on his part to be boorish' (i.e. his being rude was, ironically, a courtesy). Villano is an evaluative noun/adjective 'boorish/peasant.'
segments.inferno-33-g174: Ahi is the exclamation 'alas.' Genovesi is the vocative/plural name 'Genoese people' addressed directly; the comma shows direct address.
segments.inferno-33-g175: Uomini diversi is 'men different/various' (diversi modifying uomini). D'ogne costume = di + ogne (poetic for ogni) + costume: 'of every custom/manner.' E pien d'ogne magagna = 'and full of every defect/vice' (pien = short form of pieno 'full of'). The reversed adjective/poetic spellings (ogne) are characteristic of the poem's diction.
segments.inferno-33-g176: Perché non siete voi del mondo spersi? — perché = 'why.' Non siete voi is the present tense copula (voi = you plural): 'are you not.' Del mondo is a partitive/locative 'from the world' or 'of the world.' Spersi is a past participle used adjectivally ('scattered'/'dispersed') in standard meaning, but the provided sense-list does not match that meaning (hence marked below as not matched). The overall sense is rhetorical: 'why are you not dispersed from the world?'.
segments.inferno-33-g147: E perché introduces the reason ("And because..."). Tu (subject) + più volentier (comparative adverbial phrase “more willingly/with greater readiness”) modifies the verb rade (present indicative of radere, here used in the sense “wipe/scrape away” rather than literal shaving). Mi is the clitic object “me,” le ’nvetrïate lagrime is the direct object: nvetrïate (past-participle/adjective from invetriare) means “glazed/turned to glass” and dal volto = da + il = “from the face.”
segments.inferno-33-g148: Sappie is an archaic/poetic second-person form used like an imperative/subjunctive meaning “know (this).” Che begins the subordinate content clause “that ….”
segments.inferno-33-g149: Tosto che is a temporal connector = “as soon as.” L’anima is the grammatical subject “the soul.” Trade is from tradere (here active 3rd‑person form meaning “is handed over/handed off” or “betrays”), and come fec’ ïo = “as I did” (fec’ is the literary past form of fare/feci, with ïo = io for emphasis).
segments.inferno-33-g150: Il corpo suo = “his/its body” (noun + postposed possessive). L’è tolto uses the clitic l' before è (an anticipatory lo/it) plus essere + past participle tolto to form a passive/result: “it is taken” or “is taken away.” Da un demonio marks the agent in the passive (“by a demon”).
segments.inferno-33-g151: Che introduces a relative clause “who/which.” Poscia = “afterwards/thereafter.” Il (clitic) refers back to the body and governa is present indicative “governs/rules.” Mentre che = “while/so long as,” and l tempo suo tutto sia vòlto uses the present subjunctive (sia + past participle vòlto from volgere) to express the idea “until his time is completely turned/finished” (i.e. until the allotted time has run its course).
segments.inferno-33-g152: Ella is the feminine third‑person subject “she/it.” Ruina is present indicative from ruinare, meaning “falls/collapses” here. In sì fatta cisterna is a prepositional phrase “into such a cistern,” with sì fatta = “so made/such” qualifying cisterna.
segments.inferno-33-g153: E = “and.” Forse = “perhaps.” Pare = “seems/appears.” Ancor = “still.” Lo corpo suso = “the body up/above” (suso = up there). De l’ombra = “of the shade/shadow” (de = di). Che di qua dietro mi verna is a relative clause: verna (vernare) is an archaic verb “to winter/be sheltered,” so “the shade that winters/stays here behind me.”
segments.inferno-33-g154: Tu = “you.” ’L dei saper is a compressed periphrasis: dei here corresponds to an archaic/deformed form meaning “you must/ought” (modern devi), followed by the infinitive saper (“to know”); the clitic ’l = lo refers to the thing to be known. The whole phrase means “You must/ought to know it.”
segments.inferno-33-g155: Se = “if.” Tu vien = “you come” (vien = vieni/come). Pur = “just/even” (here reinforcing), mo = “now,” giuso = “down” (direction). The clause is conditional/hypothetical: “if you would now come down.”
segments.inferno-33-g156: Elli (archaic form of egli/egli/ella here meaning “he”) + è = “he is.” Ser is the honorific “Sir/Messer.” Branca Doria is the proper name; in the poem Branca is a personal name followed by the family name Doria.
segments.inferno-33-g157: E = “and.” Son (sono) + più anni poscia passati is an impersonal idiom meaning “more years have passed since…” (son = there are/there have been), poscia = “after/afterward,” passati = past participle “passed.” Ch’el fu sì racchiuso = “that he was thus confined/closed up” (fu = past historic of essere).
segments.inferno-33-g158: Io = “I.” Credo = present indicative of credere, “I believe.” The phrase is a simple first-person statement.
segments.inferno-33-g159: Diss’ is the contracted past tense of dire (dissi/diss'), “I said.” Io is the subject (repeated for emphasis), lui = “to him” (indirect object).
segments.inferno-33-g160: Che introduces the reported content “that ….” Tu = “you.” M’inganni is a verbal form meaning “you deceive me” (here a finite verb form, not the noun inganno); m’ = me. The verb form inganni is present (indicative/subjunctive shape in older orthography) meaning “you deceive.”
segments.inferno-33-g161: Ché = “for/because” introducing the reason. Branca Doria is the proper name. Non morì unquanche = “did not ever die” (morì = past historic “died”; unquanche = ever/at any time, here giving the sense “never” with the negative non).
segments.inferno-33-g162: A simple asyndetic list with e = “and.” Mangia (eat), bee (archaic spelling of beve = drinks), dorme (sleeps), veste panni (veste = wears/clothes, panni = clothes). All verbs are present indicative describing ordinary life activities.
segments.inferno-33-g120: Ond' is an archaic/contractional adverb meaning 'thereupon/then'; elli is the subject pronoun 'he' (inverted order after the adverb); a me is the indirect-object prepositional phrase 'to me' (the verb 'said' is implied).
segments.inferno-33-g121: Main clause with future: Avaccio sarai = 'you will soon be' (Avaccio an adverb 'soon'); dove introduces the subordinate locative clause. 'di ciò ti farà l'occhio la risposta' is a clause in which l'occhio is the subject, farà (future of fare) is the verb 'will make/give', ti is the indirect object 'to you', and la risposta is the direct object 'the answer' — literally 'where the eye will make you the answer about that.'
segments.inferno-33-g122: Veggendo is a gerund ('by/when perceiving' or 'beholding') that attaches circumstantially to the preceding clause; la cagion introduces the thing perceived, che begins a relative clause ('that/why'), and ’l fiato piove is the relative clause in which piove is a present verb meaning 'falls' (metaphorical 'falls/downpours').
segments.inferno-33-g123: Simple narrative clause: E 'and'; un de' tristi 'one of the wretched' (de' = dei/di + elision) modifies crosta 'crust' (the frozen surface); gridò is the remote past 'cried out' (3rd sg. passato remoto); a noi is the indirect object 'to us'.
segments.inferno-33-g124: Direct address: O anime crudeli = 'O/cruel souls'. 'tanto che data v’è l’ultima posta' is an explanatory/resultive clause: data is a past participle 'given', v’è (vi è) indicates 'to you there is' or 'has been to you', and l'ultima posta 'the last post/assignment' — idiomatically 'since to you the final lot has been assigned'.
segments.inferno-33-g125: Imperative plea: levatemi is an imperative with enclitic object pronoun ('lift/remove for me'), dal viso = 'from (my) face' (dal = da + il), i duri veli = direct object 'the hard veils' (i = plural article).
segments.inferno-33-g126: Result/purpose clause marked by sì ch' = 'so that' or 'so as to': ïo sfoghi is a subjunctive or hortatory-like form 'that I may vent'; ’l duol is the thing to be vented; che introduces the relative clause 'which my heart impregnates/fills' (m'impregna = 'pervades me').
segments.inferno-33-g127: Adverbial phrase 'un poco' = 'a little' (modifies the verb phrase that follows).
segments.inferno-33-g128: Temporal subordinate introduced by pria che = 'before'; ’l pianto si raggeli uses raggeli (subjunctive) meaning 'should congeal/freeze' (si is reflexive/impersonal), so the clause means 'before the weeping freezes/congeals.'
segments.inferno-33-g129: Per ch' io a lui is a short speech formula 'wherefore/so I to him:' (Per ch' = 'for which/wherefore'); word order is inverted because the spoken clause follows.
segments.inferno-33-g130: Conditional: Se vuo' = 'If you want' (vuo' = vuoi, 2nd sg. truncated), ch' i' ti sovvegna = 'that I should remember you' — ch' introduces a subordinate clause; sovvegna is present-subjunctive-like form meaning 'come to mind/remember' with i' as subject and ti as object.
segments.inferno-33-g131: Imperative request: dimmi = 'tell me'; chi se' = 'who you are' (se' is an archaic form corresponding to sei 'you are').
segments.inferno-33-g132: Coordinated conditional: e s'io non ti disbrigo = 'and if I do not free/untangle you' (s'io = se io 'if I'); disbrigo is present indicative 'I get (you) out/free you' or 'I manage matters' with ti as object.
segments.inferno-33-g133: Impersonal/final construction: al fondo de la ghiaccia = 'to the bottom of the ice'; ir mi convegna = literally 'to go becomes fitting for me' — convegna is impersonal here meaning 'it is necessary/fits me to go', with ir (infinitive) and the dative mi.
segments.inferno-33-g134: Rispuose is the passato remoto 'he replied/answered'; adunque is an adverb 'then/thereupon' connecting the speech back to the narrative.
segments.inferno-33-g135: Self-identification: I' son = 'I am' (I' is emphatic subject pronoun), frate Alberigo is his name/title (frate = brother/friar); word order is subject pronoun + copula + predicate.
segments.inferno-33-g136: I' son quel da le frutta del mal orto = 'I am he who is of the fruits of the bad garden' — quel is demonstrative 'that one/he', da introduces source/origin, del mal orto 'of the ill/bad orchard' (a proverbial formulation meaning one who produced bad fruit).
segments.inferno-33-g137: Relative clause: che qui riprendo dattero per figo = 'who here take a date for a fig' — riprendo is present 'I take/receive/take back' used transitively with dattero (a date) as direct object and per figo 'in place of a fig' indicating exchange or mistaken substitution.
